steps.title: Identify the logical gap
steps.content: Impact believers claim the Greenland crater supports their theory. But Kjær's team cannot yet determine the crater's age. To support the Younger Dryas impact theory, the crater would need to date to the start of that period — and since the age is unknown, that connection cannot be confirmed.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — it can't be concluded that the impact that made the crater was connected to the beginning of the Younger Dryas.: The team cannot date the crater, so they cannot confirm it is linked to the Younger Dryas — this follows directly from the unknown age. ✓
  • Choice B — it can't be determined whether a comet fragment could make a crater as large as 19 miles wide.: The passage does not question the size of comet fragments or the crater-size relationship; the only uncertainty is the crater's age. ✗
  • Choice C — scientists have ignored the possibility that something other than a comet fragment could have made the crater.: The debate is about whether the crater dates to the Younger Dryas, not about what caused the crater. ✗
  • Choice D — the scientists who believe an impact caused the Younger Dryas have made incorrect assumptions about when the period began.: The text presents the Younger Dryas dates (11,700–12,900 years ago) as established fact, not as uncertain or disputed. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
